titleStmt.title: Stability and Character of Zero Field Skyrmionic States in Hybrid Magnetic Multilayer Nanodots

affiliation.orgName: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Division of Physics and Applied Physics
School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences
Division of Physics and Applied Physics
School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences

analytic.title: Stability and Character of Zero Field Skyrmionic States in Hybrid Magnetic Multilayer Nanodots

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Ambient magnetic skyrmions stabilized in multilayer nanostructures are of immense interest due to their relevance to magnetic tunnel junction (MTJ) devices for memory and unconventional computing applications. However, existing skyrmionic nanostructures built using conventional metallic or oxide multilayer nanodots are unable to concurrently fulfill the requirements of nanoscale skyrmion stability and feasibility of all-electrical readout and manipulation. Here, we develop a few-repeat hybrid multilayer platform consisting of metallic [Pt/CoB/Ir]3 and oxide [Pt/CoB/MgO] components that are coupled to evolve together as a single, composite stack. Zero-field (ZF) skyrmions with sizes as small as 50 nm are stabilized in the hybrid multilayer nanodots, which are smoothly modulated by up to 2.5× by varying CoB thickness and dot sizes. Meanwhile, skyrmion multiplets are also stabilized by small bias fields. Crucially, we observe higher order "target" skyrmions with varying magnetization rotations in moderately-sized, low-anisotropy nanodots. These results provide a viable route to realize long-sought skyrmionic MTJ devices and new possibilities for multi-state skyrmionic device concepts.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: 1. Magnetic skyrmions are nanoscale spin textures with fixed handedness, or chirality 1,2 . They can be stabilized at room temperature in commercially viable multilayered magnetic thin films [3][4][5] , wherein they exhibit efficient current-induced motion 6 , nucleation 7 , and topological dynamics 8,9 .

These device attributes are promising for applications in nextgeneration memory [10][11][12] , logic [13][14][15] , and computing technologies [16][17][18] . The stability and character of chiral spin textures are governed by the interplay of conventional and chiral magnetic interactions, with the latter arising from heavy metal (HM) -ferromagnet (FM) interfaces 5,19,20 . Within nanoscale device geometries, confinement effects considerably influence their formation and evolution 3,21,22 . A comprehensive picture of skyrmion stability in ultra-thin nanodots is especially crucial for robust realizations of highly soughtafter magnetic tunnel junctions (MTJs) towards allelectrical readout and manipulation 2,23 . 2. Early works on confining nanoscale skyrmions predominantly utilized numerous stacked repetitions of metallic multilayers ([HM/FM/HM] N , N > 10) 4,6,22,24 , wherein texture stability is aided considerably by interlayer dipolar interactions 25 . In contrast, MTJ compatibility requires an ultrathin metallic multilayer, capped with a single oxide layer 23 . However, chiral nanodots comprising a single oxide trilayer (HM/FM/oxide) nucleate skyrmions only over a narrow window of magnetic and geometric parameters 3,26 , with limited utility for scalable MTJ development. In contrast, for few-repeat (N ≤ 3) metallic multilayers, spin texture stability is considerably enhanced through interlayer exchange coupling (IEC) 27,28 , with the added ability to generate strong spin-orbit torques for efficient manipulation 29,30 . This motivates case for nanostructures comprising metallic and oxide multilayers, to realize stable skyrmions with electrical detection, and efficient dynamics.

In this work, we develop few-repeat hybrid chiral multilayer nanodots by combining metallic (Pt/CoB/Ir) and oxide (Pt/CoB/MgO) stacks, and engineering them to evolve together. Using magnetometry, magnetic force microscopy (MFM), and micromagnetic simulations, we show that such nanodots behave as one composite stack, with chiral spin textures stabilized over a wide range of FM thicknesses and dot sizes, including at ZF. The spin textures exhibit a variety of configurations, including single, multiple and higher-order skyrmions, and exhibit marked variation with magnetic parameters, geometry, and external field. This provides a strategic platform to integrate the benefits of metallic and oxide multilayer stacks, with immediate relevance to functional spintronic devices.

Hybrid Multilayer Structure 4. Double wedged multilayers with active stack composition [Pt(1)/CoB(1.6-2.2) /Ir(1)] 3 /[Pt(1)/CoB(1.0-1.4)/MgO(2.6)] (thickness in nm in parentheses) were sputter-deposited onto 8" Si/SiO 2 wafers using the Singulus Timaris TM ultra-high vacuum magnetron sputtering tool (see SI §S1). The films were patterned into dots with diameters, W of 300-1000 nm using electron beam lithography (Elionix EX10 TM ) and ion beam etching (Oxford CAIBE TM ) (see SI §S2). Magnetization hysteresis loops were measured using the MicroMag Model 2900 TM alternating gradient magnetometer (see SI §S1). The chirality, manifested as the interfacial Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (iDMI), was measured using Brillouin light scat- tering (BLS) spectroscopy, performed on a wave vector resolved set-up with a six-pass tandem Fabry-Perot interferometer (see SI §S1). Spin textures were imaged by MFM performed using the Bruker Dimension ICON TM microscope with Nanosensors SSS-MFMR TM tips (diameter < 30 nm, ultra-low magnetization ∼80 emu/cm 3 ), including with in situ OP magnetic fields (0-60 mT) (see SI §S3). Finally, to interpret the experiments, grain-free micromagnetic simulations using the effective medium approximation 6 were performed with mumax 3 program 31 , using magnetic parameters, stack geometry, and dot sizes consistent with experi-ments (see SI §S4).

Our multilayer films (schematic: Fig. 1(a)) combine two distinct chiral stacks -a metallic stack [Pt/CoB(x)/Ir] 3 (represented as CoB M (x)) and oxide component [Pt/CoB(y)/MgO] (CoB O (y)). The 3repeat chiral CoB M stack was shown to host skyrmions with tunable properties at film-level 32,33 , and enables their spin-orbit torque driven nucleation and dynamics in devices 29,30 . Meanwhile, the CoB O multilayer is capped with MgO, which can serve as a tunnel barrier for electrical detection of chiral spin textures [34][35][36][37] . The two component stacks are integrated by a metallic Ir(1)/Pt(1) spacer, which is known to exhibit strong IEC 28,38 .

6. The stack components were carefully engineered to ensure their evolution as one chiral multilayer. First, magnetometry measurements were performed to determine the out-of-plane (OP) saturation fields, H s , of the individual CoB M and CoB O , in each case, over a range of CoB thicknesses (CoB M : 1.6-2.2 nm; CoB O : 1.0-1.4 nm). These measurements were used to calibrate and match pairs of CoB M and CoB O stacks with similar H s values (see SI §S1). For example, in Fig. 1 4) stack (green), which exhibits the distinctive shear characteristic of chiral multilayers. Meanwhile, wave-vector resolved BLS spectroscopy measurements, shown in Fig. 1(c) for CoB M (2.2) and CoB O (1.4), establish the left-handed Néel chirality of both stack components. The linear dispersion of the counter propagating spin-waves (Fig. 1(d)) was used to determine the iDMI, D 39 , found to be 0.42 mJ/m 2 (for CoB M (2.2)) and 1.06 mJ/m 2 (for CoB O (1.4)), respectively. The magnitude and sign of the iDMI values are in line with published reports on similar metallic 40,41 and oxide 42,43 multilayers, and are sufficiently large to stabilize homochiral spin textures 28 .

The stability and characteristics of chiral spin textures in multilayers are determined by the interplay of symmetric exchange, A, iDMI, and effective anisotropy, K eff 5,20 , each of which may vary across the wedged multilayer. For this work, D, quantified for the respective stack components in Fig. 1(d), is treated as approximately constant across the wedge, as the CoB thickness varies by < 30% for both stack components 44 . Similarly, A is also expected to vary negligibly, given the < 10% variation in the measured saturation magnetization, M s across CoB thicknesses 45 . In contrast, the K eff should be readily tuned with varying CoB thickness 32 . Indeed, the M (H) loops in Fig. 1(e) show that with increasing CoB thickness, the easy axis of the hybrid stack transitions from OP to in-plane (IP) orientation. The resulting K eff is modulated over -0.03-0.126 MJ/m 3 The nanodots exhibited five distinct magnetic states at ZF, identified as uniform magnetization (UM), skyrmion(s) (SK), labyrinthine stripes (LS), mixed-state (MIX), and target skyrmions (TSK). While the first three states (UM, SK, LS) are wellestablished 6,22 , a mixed state reflects the presence of both SK and LS states.

Meanwhile, target skyrmions are higher-order skyrmions that appear as annular or concentric domains, discussed in detail subsequently 46,47 . As seen in Fig. 2(a 2(a): bottom to top), reducing W results in transitions from LS, MIX, or TSK states to the SK state, observed for W ∼ 400 -600 nm. As W is further reduced, the dot eventually transitions to the UM state at ZF. 9. To elucidate the ZF stability of skyrmions confined in multilayer nanodots, we note that the relevant tuning parameter, K eff , includes contributions from magneto-crystalline anisotropy, shape anisotropy (K s ), and interface anisotropy (K i ) 48 . A suitably moderate K eff required for ZF skyrmion stability can be achieved by modulating the counteracting effects of reducing W , for larger K s , and increasing CoB, for smaller K i , respectively. To this end, we statistically examine the stability of the varied magnetic states across compositions and W , shown in Fig. 2(b), obtained by characterizing ∼18 nominally identical nanodots for each composition and W (see SI §3). First, for CoB M (1.8)/CoB O (1.1) (Fig. 2(b): bottom), the high K i is inherently unfavourable for stabilizing skyrmions 5,20 , and thus the sparse LS or mixed configurations directly transition to a stable UM configuration at a relatively large W ≲ 600 nm. As K i is decreased (Fig. 2(b): bottom to top), LS, mixed, and TSK states are stabilized at large W ≳ 700 nm, with greater preference for mixed over LS states in comparison to film-level magnetic textures. For these compositions, the modulation of K s with W enables the stabilization of ZF skyrmions for intermediate W (400-600 nm), wherein the required K eff is realized. Skyrmion nucleation probabilities of up to 50% are observed in 400-500 nm-sized dots with optimal compositions. Notably, the optimal window for ZF skyrmion stability shifts to smaller W , and increases in extent with increasing CoB thickness, in line with the expected modulation of K s and K i respectively.

10. Next, we turn to our striking observation of target skyrmions, which present as concentric domains, stabilized by confined geometries 46 . We define R M to be the cumulative rotation of the magnetization vector as one traces from the center of the skyrmion radially to infinity. Target skyrmions, by definition, have R M > π 46,47,49 , compared to R M = π for a skyrmion 1 . Target skyrmions have recently been observed in nanodisks of several crystalline helimagnetic systems known to host Bloch skyrmions 47,[50][51][52] . However, within multilayers, there are no reports of their inherent stability, notwithstanding structural imprinting achieved using underlying vortex structures 49 . 11. Here we observe ZF target skyrmions with R M of 2π and ∼300 nm sizes inherently stabilized in low-K inanodots of W ≳ 700 nm (Fig. 2(a): top right), and occasionally, with higher-order R M (3π, i.e. two concentric rings) as well. The nucleation of TSKs in our hybrid multilayer nanodots, and their absence in previous such works, can be attributed to several key factors: (a) the variation of magnetization between the distinct, albeit coupled CoB M and CoB O stacks 47 , (b) low interfacial anisotropy 49 , and (b) the presence of moderate geometric confinement, and (d) few-repeat stack geometry 53 , the confluence of which likely coerce the elongated chiral stripes to coil onto themselves, or induce the nucleation of a smaller skyrmion within an oppositely-polarized skyrmion. While absent in corresponding thin films, such target skyrmions are nucleated with ∼10-15% probability at optimal dot sizes, against LS and mixed states. Their optimal stability at W ∼ 700 nm dots of thick CoB, suggest that, analogous to conventional skyrmions, their stability can be attributed to a window of K eff , arising from moderately large W , and the low K i of thick CoB. These con-ditions may be required to support the in-plane curling critical for the stability of higher-order skyrmions relative to LS and mixed states. 12. To elucidate the interplay of confinement and anisotropy effects on skyrmion stability in our hybrid multilayer nanodots, we performed grain-free micromagnetic simulations using the effective medium approximation. The stack structures and magnetic parameters were chosen to emulate their experimentally studied counterparts (CoB M (1.6-2.2)/CoB O (1.0-1.4)) and the ZF states of nanodots were studied for a range of W of 400-600 nm (Fig. 3, see SI §S4). As seen in experiments, simulations support the stability of TSKs in nanodots with near-zero or negative K eff (Fig. 3: top half). Meanwhile for dots with intermediate K eff (CoB M (1.9)/CoB O (1.2)-equivalent), we observe a multi-state transition with reducing W , from TSK to SK to UM states. Finally, for intermediate values of W and K eff , the simulations suggest the stability of skyrmionic multiplets, which have been experimentally observed in nanodots with higher repetitions 22 . While the simulations qualitatively reproduce the experimentally observed trends of skyrmion stability evolution with W and K i , we note quantitative discrepancies that inhibit one-to-one comparisons with experiment. One likely source is the imprecise magnitude of interlayer exchange coupling (IEC), both within the CoB M stack and between the CoB M -CoB O . In contrast to multilayers with high stack repetitions, where dipolar effects assume greater importance 25 , IEC is known to influence the stability of ZF spin textures in few-repeat multilayers 27,28,54 . Future simulation works may build on accurately determined IECs, e.g., using bespoke magnetometry techniques 28,54,55 , to quantitatively elucidate transitions between SK and TSK states, towards their practical utilization.

div.head: Skyrmion Size Dependence
div.p: 13. Next, we examine the evolution of skyrmion stability with in situ magnetic field (µ 0 H). Fig. 4(a)

shows the MFM-imaged magnetic states for representative CoB M (2.0)/CoB O (1.3) nanodots as a function of W and µ 0 H. Expectedly, the W ≃ 300 nm dots remain uniform throughout, and W ≃ 400 nm dots consistently form a single skyrmion over the field range. Meanwhile, for the larger 500 and 600 nm dots, a small OP field is sufficient to destabilize LS and TSK states, giving way to skyrmion multiplets over moderate fields (20-50 mT), which diminish to single skyrmions at higher fields en route to saturation. Finally, the size of skyrmions, d S , is also visibly influenced by the applied field, stack properties, and geometry, as detailed below.

14. Fig. 4(b) shows the evolution of ZF skyrmion size for dot sizes, W ≃ 400, 500 nm, previously shown to host skyrmions across most samples (see Fig. 2, SI §S3). The ZF d S goes as low as ∼ 50 nm and varies by up to 2.5× with CoB thicknesses. Expectedly, for larger W , the measured d S is greater, and exhibits reduced sensitivity to sample composition 22,53 .

Meanwhile, the linear increase in ZF sizes of isolated skyrmions with decreasing K eff (increasing CoB thickness) is not reported in any other system to the best of our knowledge. In this regard, our prior work on high-repeat multilayer nanodots, which reported a direct correlation between d S and D/A for skyrmion multiplets, also included an implicit direct correlation with K eff , and therefore involved a complex interplay of multiple parameters and skyrmion interactions. In contrast, the large (2.5×) tunability of ZF d S achieved here with a single-magnetic knob (K eff ) offers greater utility towards functional devices.

15. Finally, we examine the field-dependence of d S for the case of moderate K eff (CoB M (2.0)/CoB O (1.3)) by imaging 9 nominally identical nanodots for each field and W (Fig. 4(c)). Expectedly, smaller skyrmions are observed with increasing µ 0 H, with the average d S across dots decreasing from ∼ 85 nm to ∼ 65 nm. While smaller dots (W ≲ 500 nm) hosting individual skyrmions exhibit consistent and sizable H-variation, d S is found to be relatively independent of W for larger dots (W ≳ 500 nm). Consistent with our previous work 22 (albeit for different stack parameters), the Windependence emerges at these dot sizes as a result of the emergence of skyrmion multiplets at finite fields, due to which the role of geometric confinement effects is diminished. Overall, these results demonstrate that the sub-100 nm ZF skyrmions stabilized in our hybrid multilayer nanodots exhibit consistent field evolution trends driven by stack properties and confinement ef-fects.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: 16. In summary, we report on chiral nanostructures harnessing a bespoke few-repeat hybrid multilayer, comprising metallic ([Pt/CoB(x)/Ir] 3 ) and oxide (Pt/CoB(y)/MgO) stack components, both designed with matched switching fields, and strongly coupled by IEC. The ZF magnetic states of the nanodots are determined by the interplay of dot-sizeinduced confinement effects and CoB-thickness-driven stack anisotropy. Smaller dots (W ≲ 600 nm) stabilize ZF skyrmions over a substantial range of K eff and dot sizes. The skyrmion size, as small as 50 nm at ZF, can be tuned by up to 2.5× by engineering the stack anisotropy and geometric parameters. Finally, large-W , low-K eff dots host higher-order target skyrmions at ZF with chiral rotation up to 3π, whose stability relative to skyrmionic multiplets can be tailored by small bias fields realizable using stack engineering. 17. The presented stability of ZF skyrmions in our few-repeat hybrid multilayer nanodots has practical consequences for several efforts on chiral spin textures. On one hand, the inherent realization of higher-order skyrmions and their interplay with conventional skyrmions in highly-scalable multilayer nanodots is expected to enable their much-sought functionalization via device concepts harnessing topological transitions 18 . Notably, the stability of conventional ZF skyrmions over a wide range of magnetic and geometric parameters within MTJ-compatible hybrid multilayer nanodots is of critical importance, given their limited stability in prior MTJ-compatible stacks 3,26 . Our hybrid stack provides a viable platform for realizing electrical skyrmion readout and manipulation 55 , and can be generalized to other materials and geometries, notably including racetracks, to achieve all-electrical skyrmionics 2,23 .

figure.figDesc: (b), the H s for CoB M (2.2) (blue) and CoB O (1.4) (red) is ∼180 mT. Finally, the double-wedged composite stack was fabricated using matched CoB M and CoB O thicknesses to ensure coherent switching of the hybrid multilayer. Fig. 1(b) shows the resulting OP magnetization hysteresis loop, M (H), of the CoB M (2.2)/CoB O (1.

figure.head: 1 FIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2. Confined Zero-field (ZF) Spin Textures. (a) Magnetic force microscopy (MFM) images (scalebar: 100 nm) at ZF of spin texture configurations in nanodots of varying sizes (W , left-to-right), and for varying multilayer compositions (i-to-iv). Background color indicates identified state of dot: uniform magnetization (UM, grey), skyrmion(s) (SK, green), labyrinthine stripes (LS, blue), mixed LS, SK (MIX, teal), and target skyrmion (TSK, yellow), respectively. (b) Empirically determined ZF probability of stabilizing the respective states for varying multilayers and dot sizes, determined by averaging over 18 nominally identical dots for each presented dataset.

Fig. 1(f)), and the OP-IP crossover (K eff ∼ 0) occurs between samples CoB M (1.9)/CoB O (1.2) and CoB M (2.0)/CoB O (1.3). Skyrmion Stability Fig. 2(a) shows representative ZF MFM images of our nanodots across varying CoB M (1.8-2.2)/CoB O (1.1-1.4) thicknesses and W over 300-1000 nm.

), these ZF magnetic states evolve systematically with varying CoB thickness, and W . First, for the thinnest CoB sample -CoB M (1.6)/CoB O (1.0) (not shown) the nanodots display UM states across all W , with the preferential stability of single domains arising from the substantially larger film-level K eff (Fig.1(f)) compared to the other samples. Next, for CoB M (1.8)/CoB O (1.1) nanodots (Fig. 2(a): bottom), reducing W results in a sharp transition from LS to UM states, albeit without any intermediate states. As the CoB thickness is further increased across CoB M (1.9-2.2)/CoB O (1.2-1.4) (Fig.

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. Simulated ZF Spin Textures in Nanodots.Micromagnetic simulations of OP magnetization (mz, scale bar: 100 nm) for nanodots with varying W (left-to-right), and varying multilayer-equivalent effective stack parameters (i-to-iv). Background colour indicates identified state of dot: UM (grey), SK (green), and TSK (yellow), respectively.

figure.head: FIG. 4 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 4.Magnetic Field Dependence of Confined Skyrmion Properties. (a) MFM images of CoBM(2.0)/CoBO(1.3) nanodots (scale bar: 100 nm) for varying in situ OP fields (µ0H, left-to-right, acquired as independent datasets) and with varying W (bottom-to-top). Background color indicates identified state of dot (see Fig.2(a)). (b) Average size of skyrmions (dS, from 18 nominally identical nanodots for each size), stabilized at ZF in W ≃ 400, 500 nm dots, for varying multilayer compositions (K eff reduces right-to-left). Dashed lines are linear fits. (c) Field-evolution of average dS (from 9 nominally identical nanodots for each size) for sample CoBM(2.0)/CoBO(1.3) as a function of µ0H for all W . Dashed line is a linear fit to the average dS evolution.

div.p: Acknowledgments. This work was supported by the SpOT-LITE programme (Grant No. A18A6b0057), funded by Singapore's RIE2020 initiatives, and by NUS funds (Grant No. A-0004544-00-00).

monogr.title: All-electrical skyrmionic bits in a chiral magnetic tunnel junction
